Social Democracy: An Alternate History
1929
A new year
A new year begins. The economy appears to be slackening; unemployment is increasing throughout the republic, although for the most part, people are still doing well.
1930
A new year
A new year begins. The economic crisis is worsening throughout the world, with no end in sight. The <span style="color: #7A3C00;">
NSDAP
</span> seems to be strengthening in local elections. All very worrisome trends.
1931
A new year
A new year begins. 
Unemployment continues to spiral upwards. The strength of the <span style="color: #7A3C00;">
NSDAP
</span> continues to rise. 
A works program has been enacted in order to reduce unemployment. 
However, unemployment remains too high for comfort.
1932
A new year
A new year begins. 
There is little to no good news in Germany. The economic depression has still not bottomed out. 
We have begun to launch a public works program, but unemployment is still stubbornly high. The people are beginning to finally have optimism, however. 
We have begun to launch a public works program, and unemployment is finally starting to fall. The people are beginning to finally have optimism. 
The presidential election is in April of this year.
1933
A new year
A new year begins. 
There is finally some good news in Germany. The economic depression has begun the early stages of global recovery. 
We have begun to launch a public works program, but unemployment is still stubbornly high. The people are beginning to finally have optimism, however, with the global recovery beginning. 
We have begun to launch a public works program, and unemployment is finally starting to fall. The people are beginning to finally have optimism. The depression has begun the early stages of recovery across the world as well.
1934
A new year
1934
A new year begins. 
The economic depression seems to have bottomed out, but there is still much suffering. It is not too late to launch an economic rescue program. 
We have begun to launch a public works program, but the economy is still troubled. The recovery has begun, but more must be done. 
Our public works program has successfully guided us through the worst of the crisis. The economy is improving, and finally people have optimism for the future. 
With the return to normalcy, the political landscape in Germany is shifting. Liberalism is making a return among the middle classes, and the radical parties of the right are declining. 
The president is in poor health; he is not expected to live out the year.
Back

End game.
The <span style="color: #7A3C00;">Nazi</span> Beast Tamed?
Ending 0.5
With the appointment of 
 and the passage of the Enabling Act, Germany is lost. Schleicher, increasingly sidelined by health issues, fades into irrelevance. Following
&nbsp;President Hindenburg's and
 his
&nbsp;own
 death, General Werner von Blomberg assumes the presidency, marking the end of the failed taming experiment.
The bourgeois establishment that once collaborated with the <span style="color: #7A3C00;">Nazis</span> is purged en masse during the Night of the Long Knives and offer no serious resistance thereafter, aside from the failed Oster conspiracy in 1938 by former Schleicher loyalists.

Economics
Inflation: 
%<br> 
Unemployment: 
% 
<br> Economic growth: 
%
<div class="scrollable-box">
<p class="both">
It is a bright time for German democracy. After a period of struggle, inflation is finally under control, and the economy is expanding steadily. In foreign policy, we have reached agreements with the Western powers and the <span style="color: #750e0e;">Soviet Union</span> that might make genuine peace a reality. <br><br> Now, the task of <span style="color: #c00000;">Social Democracy</span> is to prepare for the next election. The <span style="color: #000000;">bourgeois</span>-<span style="color: #3E88B3;">right</span> government of Wilhelm Marx is unpopular, and we stand to gain. Perhaps the next government will have a <span style="color: #c00000;">socialist</span> majority. 
</p>
<p class="both">
The current <span style="color: #000000;">bourgeois</span>-<span style="color: #3E88B3;">right</span> government has fallen. The next elections are slated for May of this year. 
</p>
<p class="dnvp">
Special interest parties such as the <span style="color: #b8996f;">Reich Party of the Middle Class</span>âcommonly referred to as the <span style="color: #b8996f;">Economic Party</span>(<span style="color: #b8996f;">
WP
</span>)âand the <span style="color: #7FCEB1;">Christian-National Peasants' and Farmers' Party</span>(<span style="color: #7FCEB1;">
CNBLP
</span>) are expected to gain a decent foothold in the Reichstag, due to the belief that the established political parties are neglecting their interests. This negatively impacts the <span style="color: #3E88B3;">
DNVP
</span>, having been part of the last government. 
</p>
<p class="other">
The right-wing industrial forces in the <span style="color: #C0A054;">People's Party</span>(<span style="color: #C0A054;">
DVP
</span>) has been gaining considerable influence within the party due to party leader and Foreign Minister Gustav Stresemann's neglect of internal party affairs. The prospect of a Grand Coalition with the <span style="color: #C0A054;">
DVP
</span> is becoming increasingly difficult, especially with Stresemann's declining health. 
</p>
<p class="other">
Our neighbor <span style="color: #002654;">France</span> has held its legislative elections, resulting in a defeat for the <span style="color: #FFC0C0;">Left Cartel</span>, and the continuation of right-wing cabinets. 
</p>
<p class="other">
The voting age for women in <span style="color: #C8102E;">Great Britain</span> has been lowered from 30 to 21, another day another banger. 
</p>
<p class="dnvp">
A round of internal in-fighting has arisen within the <span style="color: #3E88B3;">
DNVP
</span> following their poor results in the last election. A controversial article from Walther Lambach, a prominent member of the "<span style="color: #6D5298;">Christian Social</span>" wing of the party, blames the results on the talk of restoring of the monarchy as unrealistic and calls for the party to open up to conservative <span style="color: #000000;">rep</span><span style="color: #DD0000;">ubl</span><span style="color: #FFCC00;">icans</span>. 
Furthermore, their participation in government in the system they're fundamentally opposed has created a rift with the radical right elements within their party. 
His controversial take has surprisingly found some support within the party. 
Alfred Hugenberg and his allies on the right of the party are seeking to capitalize on monarchist anger against Lambach to seize control in the party congress this October. 
</p>
<p class="other">
The poor showing for the liberal parties has convinced their leadership that Germany no longer has the luxury of two liberal parties. However, the prospect of a merger is held back by the left <span style="color: #D3C24D;">
</span> and the right <span style="color: #C0A054;">
DVP
</span>, who are strongly against compromising their own interests. Despite this, a parliamentary group between the <span style="color: #D3C24D;">
</span> and <span style="color: #C0A054;">
DVP
</span> is seeming likely. 
</p>
<p class="dnvp">
Kuno von Westarp, the moderate leader of the <span style="color: #3E88B3;">
DNVP
</span>, has resigned as party leader following conflict with Hugenberg and his allies. Westarp, although opposed to Lambach's views, refrained from expelling him from the party to prevent a possible secession from the party's left wing, to the outrage of many monarchists. 
This has prompted Westarp to resign as chairman, and Lambach to be expelled from the party due to unrelenting attacks from Hugenberg's allies. 
This has prompted Westarp to resign as chairman due to unrelenting attacks from Hugenberg's allies, but Lambach is saved from expulsion by the <span style="color: #3E88B3;">
DNVP
</span>'s left wing. 
 Meanwhile, Hugenberg and his allies in the Pan-German League have been garnering considerable support within their local party organizations. 
</p>
<p class="other">
The efforts of Stresemann and the <span style="color: #D3C24D;">
</span> under Koch-Weser to strengthen ties between their two parties have been a failure due to the strong opposition voiced by the extreme wings of both parties. Stresemann is growing disillusioned with his own party, viewing it as a vehicle for industrial interests rather than liberal values. The leftists in the <span style="color: #D3C24D;">

</p>
<p class="other">
The opposition to a <span style="color: #FFCC00;">united liberal party</span> extends beyond the right wing of the <span style="color: #C0A054;">
DVP
</span>. Many politicians of all factions within the <span style="color: #D3C24D;">
</span>, including Koch-Weser himself, have expressed interest in an alternative merger with the <span style="color: #D3D3A9;">
VNR
</span>. 
However, Koch-Weser and the business wing of the party has discredited the skeptics with the party, and will proceed with unification. 
</p>

</span>.

<p class="other">
Austria's constitution has been amended to grant the President increased powers, including the authority to dismiss parliament, along with introducing direct presidential elections by the people. This has transformed Austria into a semi-presidential system of government, not too dissimilar to our own. The amendment was a result of a compromise with the <span style="color: #000000;">Christian Social Party</span>'s push for a more authoritarian and centralized government, as they needed the <span style="color: #C8102E;">Austrian Social Democrats</span>' approval to pass the amendment. 
</p>
<p class="dnvp">
The <span style="color: #6D5298;">Christian Social</span> faction of the <span style="color: #3E88B3;">
DNVP
</span> have formed their own political party by merging with the CVD, to form the <span style="color: #8ED8E4;">Christian Social People's Service</span> (<span style="color: #8ED8E4;">
CSVD
</span>). The party mainly has support among Protestant workers, and refuses to cooperate with other bourgeois parties. They are surprisingly taking a hard stance towards the <span style="color: #7A3C00;">Nazis</span>, decrying their "un-Christian excessive hatred of <span style="color: #005EB8;">Jews</span>". 
</p>
<p class="dnvp">
The <span style="color: #3E88B3;">
DNVP
</span> secessionists have joined forces with the <span style="color: #7FCEB1;">Christian-National Peasants' and Farmers' Party</span>(<span style="color: #7FCEB1;">

</span>) to establish the Christian-National Coalition, a new parliamentary faction in the Reichstag under the leadership of Treviranus. While this cooperation appears to signal a step toward unity among the anti-Hugenberg right, the <span style="color: #7FCEB1;">
CNBLP
</span> still claims that this alliance is merely a temporary measure driven by the parliamentary weakness of both groups. The primary barrier to further unification of the right-wing opposition remains the determination of the various parties to preserve their distinct ideological and vocational identities. 
</p>

</span> dissidents, which ended in the failure to create even a loose federation of conservative forces. The association is not yet a formal party but aims to unify the <span style="color: #8ED8E4;">CSVD</span> and <span style="color: #7FCEB1;">
CNBLP
</span> into a single bloc, hoping to field a joint list in the next election.

Spanish dictator Miguel Primo de Rivera has resigned, as the Spanish people have grown tired with the dictatorship, especially after the onset of the economic depression. King Alfonso XIII has appointed a new dictator to govern in his place, but republican forces in the fragile kingdom are gaining significant support among the people. 
</p>

DNVP
</span>. Schiele has proposed a plan to form a new agrarian party by merging the RLB with the <span style="color: #7FCEB1;">

</span>, aiming for it to serve as a sister party to the <span style="color: #0087DC;">People's Conservatives</span>, a party that would have greater appeal to urban areas. This proposal has been shot down by the old guard of the <span style="color: #7FCEB1;">
CNBLP
</span>, who are adamantly opposed to the reestablishment of agriculture to an ideologically oriented party.

<p class="other">

This has led to heightened tensions between BrÃ¼ning and his supporting parties, some of whom are even threatening to abandon his coalition.

This crisis presented a perfect opportunity for DollfuÃ to abolish the parliamentary system and restrict democratic rights, such as the freedom of the press and public protests. Another democracy has fell to authoritarianism. 
</p>
<p class="other">
DollfuÃ's <span style="color: #7A3C00;">Austrofascist</span> dictatorship has banned the <span style="color: #700000;">Austrian Communists</span> and the paramilitary of the <span style="color: #C8102E;">Austrian Social Democrats</span>, the <span style="color: #C8102E;">Republican Protection League</span>. He has also established the <span style="color: #7A3C00;">Fatherland Front</span>, a party promoting a far-right <span style="color: #7A3C00;">fascist</span> and corporate ideology. 
</p>
<p class="other">
The <span style="color: #EF1C27;">Spanish Socialists</span> and the <span style="color: #E25822;">Republican Left</span> have suffered an overwhelming defeat at the hands of the right wing <span style="color: #303180;">
CEDA
</span> and increasingly right-wing <span style="color: #A871A8;">Radicals</span>. 
</p>
<p class="other">
The Austrian Civil War has begun and ended in a matter of three days, fought between the <span style="color: #C8102E;">Republican Protection League</span> and Austria's <span style="color: #7A3C00;">Austrofascist</span> regime, resulting in a defeat for the <span style="color: #C8102E;">socialists</span> across the border. The uprising was sparked by efforts to suppress the remnants of the <span style="color: #C8102E;">Austrian Social Democrats</span> and their paramilitary wing. However, the poorly coordinated and weakened movement was swiftly crushed by the army. Perhaps things would have gone more differently if they have taken up arms earlier. 
</p>

Land Reform
-1 budget - Break up the large aristocratic estates and give land to landless workers.
Requires 1 budget. 
The judiciary would never allow this.
With our policy of land reform, we are acquiring farmland from large estates, and giving the land to farmworkers' associations and small peasants. In essence, this brings class struggle to the countryside, breaking the semi-feudal property relations present in East Prussia and other areas with large estates. The bourgeois parties and large farmers despise our intrusion into the sacredness of private property, while leftists are cautiously optimistic.
This policy has especially earned us the ire of President Hindenburg and his inner circle.

The pause in reparation payments, a budgetary surplus, and a struggling economy have created the conditions necessary to persuade both the BrÃ¼ning cabinet and many economic experts that a stimulus plan is essential. The economy has hit rock bottom, deflation is in its damaging second phase, and the Lautenbach Plan is considered crucial for a rapid 'ignition' of the German economy, especially as reparations have already been paused. However, due to the German public's fear of inflation, announcing any dramatic expansion of the money supply has proven impossible. As a result, the Reichsbank is forced to scale back the plan to avoid any potential scandal that could threaten the BrÃ¼ning government.
Labor Minister Adam Stegerwald and Finance Minister Hermann Dietrich have devised a plan to allocate a billion dollars for public works, along with efforts to reduce 'sticky' wages to restore investor confidence. The plan also includes the purchasing of foreign debts to increase liquidity, expanding credit, and reducing interest rates. The deficit generated by these measures is also somewhat offset by the need to pay less unemployment benefits. With these measures, money is beginning to flow once again into the German economy.

The Death of Hindenburg
Paul von Hindenburg has passed away at age 86.
The nation engages in an outpouring of grief for its war hero and former president. Even for committed <span style="color: #c00000;">socialists</span> and <span style="color: #000000;">rep</span><span style="color: #DD0000;">ubl</span><span style="color: #FFCC00;">icans</span>, this is a bittersweet moment. Economic Expansion
The economy is growing steadily.
Economic Expansion
The German economy has been growing steadily for an extended period of time, and has low inflation and unemployment. The government is rewarded by additional tax revenue from the same tax rates. Even the capitalists find that they have little to complain about when it comes to government policies. One downside of this economic growth, however, is the rising risk of inflation.
Economic Sanctions
Our military buildup has caused sanctions from France and other countries.
Economic Sanctions
In response to our military rearmament in contravention of the Versailles Treaty, the Western Allies have imposed economic sanctions on Germany. The costs of the sanctions are largely borne by the proletariat; the people are facing increasing poverty.

BrÃ¼ningâs decision to postpone the Lausanne conference until June faces sharp criticism from top economic advisors, who argue that the outright abolition of reparations would remain impossible even then. Instead, they urge him to pursue dÃ©tente and secure access to new <span style="color: #002654;">French</span> loansâan idea Hindenburg has adamantly opposed, vowing to resign rather than sign such an agreement.
BrÃ¼ning, however, has reason to believe waiting is the right move. <span style="color: #002654;">France</span> is facing a severe fiscal shortfall and remains reluctant to cut off a crucial revenue stream.
That is, if BrÃ¼ning can hold on until June.
BrÃ¼ning's Dilemma
An international reparations congress at Lausanne is scheduled this month.
BrÃ¼ning has decided to postpone reparation talks until June.
BrÃ¼ning's Dilemma
Following the disastrous fallout from the Banking Crisis, BrÃ¼ning has successfully managed to secure key concessions from the <span style="color: #0A3161;">Western</span> <span style="color: #C8102E;">Allies</span> regarding debts and Germany's economic maneuverability. However, Germany's economic crisis continues to worsen, and the Beneduce Report has stated that Germany cannot afford reparations payments in the foreseeable future. The <span style="color: #C8102E;">British</span> government has invited the <span style="color: #0A3161;">Western</span> <span style="color: #C8102E;">Allies</span> to participate in an international reparations congress in Lausanne, as per the recommendations of the bankers. 
With the moratorium on reparations in place, there is considerable uncertainty regarding what will happen to Germany's reparations burden once it expires. BrÃ¼ning argues that this uncertainty has stifled investor confidence and hindered Germany's recovery from the economic crisis. The dire state of German finances has also contributed to this, with the Beneduce Report painting a bleak picture of Germany's ability to repay reparations in the foreseeable future. The <span style="color: #C8102E;">British</span> government has invited the <span style="color: #0A3161;">Western</span> <span style="color: #C8102E;">Allies</span> to participate in an international reparations congress in Lausanne, as per the recommendations of the bankers. 
This is where the dilemma arises: <span style="color: #002654;">France</span>'s government cannot discuss any permanent revision of the Young Plan until after their elections in May. BrÃ¼ning faces a choice: he can either push for a prolongation of the moratorium on reparations for another three years, or he can wait until June for a potential complete abolition of reparations.

Lausanne Conference Begins

Schleicher's Health Deteriorates
Unbeknownst to the public, Schleicher has been suffering from pernicious anemia for several years. As early as 1930, his family doctor warned him that, given the stress and heavy responsibilities of his career, he likely had no more than six years to live. Now, with the added strain of the chancellorship, those who interact with him have begun to notice his steady decline into lethargy. He may only have a few months left.
In a public announcement, Schleicher announces his resignation, stating that he will soon handpick a successor to be appointed by Hindenburg. He never truly enjoyed the role, and in recent months has quietly delegated his duties to trusted allies in the Reichswehr, grooming them to take his place.

Reichstag Reconvenes
The Reichstag reconvenes under Schleicher, and nearly all parties present are unwilling to move against the popular chancellor on the chamberâs first day back. The opposition chooses to wait, looking for the inevitable misstepâa constitutional violation or a divisive policyâto use as a pretext and rallying point against him.

The Reichstag enters session.

Burden of Government
Burden of Government
The weight of governance now falls upon our shoulders. It is our responsibility to maintain the delicate balance of appeasing our coalition partners while advancing our party's agenda. Our voter base consists of a broad array of different classes, but mainly consists of the working-class and the unemployed. They expect us to enact the following policies during our tenure, or we will suffer consequences. 
So far, ceding control of the Labor Ministry to the bourgeois parties has left a poor impression with the workers. 
Primary Goals
- Expand social welfare programs. 
- Promote worker ownership of industries. 
- Lower taxes or shift the tax burden to the wealthy. 
- Support workers in labor disputes. 
- Fund unemployment insurance through employers. 
- Advance women's rights. 
- Reform or expand education for workers. 
- Institute an economic plan. 
- Secure low food prices for workers. 
- Reduce funding for the Reichswehr. 
Secondary Goals
- Promote the moderate reformist plan. 
- Cut taxes. 
- Be neutral in labor affairs. 
- Raise tariffs. 
- Increase science funding. 
- Take steps towards the <span style="color: #001489;">European</span> <span style="color: #FFDD00;">Union</span> or national concordat. 
- Assist the rural working class. 
They will expect to see at least two primary and one secondary goal fulfilled every six months, or we will lose some of their support. Committing to austerity or conservative policies will automatically fail the primary goal. Committing to radical left-wing policies will automatically fail the secondary goal.

The Perfect Dictatorship
Ending 1
During 
âs term as President, the
Following the death of Hindenburg from old age and Schleicher due to health complications, General Hammerstein is elected in a landslide in a heavily rigged election. The
 hated <span style="color: #000000;">We</span><span style="color: #DD0000;">im</span><span style="color: #FFCC00;">ar</span> system is dismantled, and a new March Constitution is passed near-unanimously. The Reichstag is reduced to a rubber stamp for the regime. Germany formally adopts a presidential system, with both the Presidency and Chancellorship held by various <span style="color: #BFC8CC;">
DNEF
</span> members, who rise through bribes, patronage, and backroom deals. Corruption is endemic. Elections are stage-managed, dominated by fraud and manipulation.
The <span style="color: #c00000;">
SPD
</span> is never formally banned and endures as a powerless "loyal opposition," unable to influence national policy. Though tolerated, they face constant harassment and suppression by the ruling regime.
Despite the regimeâs authoritarianism, social discontent remains subdued. A <i>Wirtschaftswunder</i>âan economic miracleâemerges as the Great Depression fades. In 1935, Germany secures equality in armaments at Geneva and begins agitating for the return of the Polish Corridor separating East Prussia from the Reich.
In 1942, a "special military operation" is launched to seize Danzig. Poland refuses to cede territory, and with backing from <span style="color: #002654;">France</span> and a hesitant <span style="color: #C8102E;">Britain</span>, enters a state of war. A puppet regime is installed in occupied territories. A limited <span style="color: #002654;">French</span> offensive into the Saar fails, and the <span style="color: #0A3161;">Western</span> <span style="color: #C8102E;">Allies</span> remain at war only on paper.
Sensing German isolation, the <span style="color: #750e0e;">Soviet Union</span> invades eastern Poland, citing abuses against native Belarusians and Ukrainians under military occupation. A new Germanâ<span style="color: #750e0e;">Soviet</span> war erupts. Defense Minister Hammerstein cultivates ties with the "Little Entente"âstates facing their own <span style="color: #750e0e;">Soviet</span>-backed revolutionary threats. An unofficial Mitteleuropa bloc emerges, with Germany at its core, squeezed between a Western capitalist bloc and the Eastern state-<span style="color: #c00000;">socialist</span> order.
In 1946, the war ends in stalemate. Poland is partitioned at the Vistula River. Romania and Bulgaria fall under <span style="color: #700000;">Communist</span> control. Western Europe grows closer to the <span style="color: #B31942;">United</span> <span style="color: #0A3161;">States</span>, and a tripolar global order takes shape.
By the late 1960s, economic stagnation and renewed domestic unrestâdriven by the Red Army Faction and neo-Nazi agitationâforce Chancellor Kurt Georg Kiesinger to begin relaxing the dictatorship. The corporatist house of cards that had maintained the regime's stability for decades begins to crumble as independent unions discreetly form. However, Germany remains far from a free and democratic country, the Reichswehr maintains constant watch over politics, exercising real control behind the scenes.
Today, the <span style="color: #BFC8CC;">
DNEF
</span> operates as a conventional big-tent political party. Though still marred by corruption, it remains the third major force in German politics, behind the <span style="color: #000000;">
CVP
</span> and <span style="color: #d24a00;">
VSPD
</span>. The Mitteleuropa bloc persists as a fragile intermediary between the West and the Eastâneither liberal nor <span style="color: #c00000;">socialist</span>, but something in between.
The German Republic, called by some of our detractors as the "<span style="color: #000000;">We</span><span style="color: #DD0000;">im</span><span style="color: #FFCC00;">ar</span> Republic", is a parliamentary republic with a somewhat weak president and a strong parliament, called the Reichstag. The Reichstag is responsible for governing, while the President has a largely ceremonial role but also has some important governing responsibilities.
The Reichstag is elected based on a proportional representation system, giving parties seats roughly proportional to their vote share in each electoral district. The Reichstag then elects the Chancellor and the cabinet by a majority vote, who are responsible for government. A majority vote-of-no-confidence can remove the current government.
The President is elected by a national direct vote, in two rounds. The second round is used if the first round does not result in a majority for one candidate; the candidate who receives a plurality in the second round becomes president (there is no obligation for weaker candidates to drop out). The role of the president is mostly to represent Germany, but he also has some crucial governing powers. The president and chancellor can together use emergency decrees to govern, bypassing the Reichstag. The president can also appoint or remove a chancellor when a parliamentary majority is unable to be established, and can thus influence the formation of a government.
Germany is divided into a number of states, each of which have their own governments with some responsibilities. By far the largest and most important state is Prussia.
Back
Dynamic Social Democracy is an ambitious overhaul mod aimed to bring historical realism and a dynamic party system to the late Weimar Republic. This mod heavily focuses on the <span style="color: #7A3C00;">Nazi Party</span>'s rise to power, and puts emphasis on the disastrous mistakes made by the parties surrounding the <span style="color: #c00000;">
SPD
</span>, which effectively enabled Adolf Hitler's eventual appointment by Hindenburg. This aspect is not represented in the base game, which instead focuses on the player's direct impact.
The Weimar party system in this mod reacts to your every move, adjusting ideologies and strategies accordingly. Hindenburg and the bourgeois parties now serve as more realistic and impactful obstacles, as they did in real life.
A real life example is the situation following the 1928 federal election, despite the <span style="color: #c00000;">
SPD
</span>'s clear electoral mandate in 1928, the <span style="color: #000000;">Center</span>, <span style="color: #C0A054;">
DVP
</span>, and Hindenburg himself initially opposed the formation of the Grand Coalition. However, Stresemann's intervention, Hindenburg's desire to use the <span style="color: #c00000;">
SPD
</span>'s anti-militarist stance to renegotiate German reparations, and his strategy to burden the <span style="color: #c00000;">
SPD
</span> with governance to decrease their popularity, is what made this unlikely arrangement possible.
What was initially seen with positivity by the <span style="color: #c00000;">
SPD
</span> at the time; their entrance into government may be one of the biggest reasons for the demise of German democracy.
By March 1930, all of the factors above were no longer in play, the line between the <span style="color: #C0A054;">
DVP
</span> and the parties on the <span style="color: #7A3C00;">far</span>-<span style="color: #3E88B3;">right</span> were blurred, the Young Plan was ratified, and the <span style="color: #c00000;">
SPD
</span> was suffering as a result of the economic depression. The appointment of Bruning under an emergency government wasn't improvised when the <span style="color: #C0A054;">
DVP
</span> took down the Grand Coalition, it has been an idea longed by Schleicher, Hindenburg, and many <span style="color: #808080;">reactionary</span> elites.
"On 6 January 1930, Hugenberg was summoned to meet President Paul von Hindenburg, who told him that now the Young Plan had been passed, he had no more need for MÃ¼ller, and he was planning to bring in a new presidential government very soon that would be "anti-<span style="color: #D3C24D;">parliamentary</span> and anti-<span style="color: #9B0000;">Marxist</span>"."
Your primary goal is to save German <span style="color: #000000;">dem</span><span style="color: #DD0000;">ocr</span><span style="color: #FFCC00;">acy</span>, saving <span style="color: #c00000;">Social Democracy</span> is secondary.

Popular among the bourgeoisie, much less so among the workers.

While a 40-hour work week was declared during the German Revolution in 1918, enforcement has become increasingly porous over the years. The capitalists have successfully added numerous exceptions and loopholes, returning to the old regime of forced overtime for workers.
Now, with the force of the Labor Ministry, we are finally enforcing the rights of workers to their own time. The bourgeoisie and their political representatives are displeased.

During these searches, a copy of a letter sent by <span style="color: #7A3C00;">

Expectedly, the Lautenbach Plan has failed due to strong opposition from those who adhere to fiscal orthodoxy, which holds that deflation, despite its pain, is healthy because it weeds out inefficient producers. This contrasts with the Keynesian belief that a destructive second phase occurs, where economic recovery becomes possible but fails because of a collapse in investor confidence.
Reichsbank President Hans Luther, who personally sympathizes with increasing the money supply and expanding credit, has shelved the idea. The conference only reached two unanimous conclusions: the German people's fear of inflation made it impossible to announce any dramatic program to expand the money supply, meaning the government would have to act secretly if it ever decided to implement Lautenbach's plan, and the government must continue its campaign to lower wages bound by long-term collective labor contracts and prices set by cartel agreements.
However, Luther has made concessions to the Keynesians, quietly expanding the money supply and increasing credit availability, even abandoning the rule that 40% of money in circulation must be covered by gold and foreign exchange. Furthermore, generous financing of exports to the <span style="color: #750e0e;">Soviet Union</span> has helped counteract the effects of the withdrawal of foreign credits on both the money supply and the job market.
"Stimulation can only help if we are already at the bottom....I do not believe that we are that far yet."
- Ernst Trendelenburg, Reich Minister of Economics, 1931.

The Lautenbach Plan
BrÃ¼ning's deflationary and austerity policy, aimed at boosting Germany's competitiveness on the global stage after the crisis
through a pause in reparations, which is BrÃ¼ning's main objective
, has shown to have disastrous consequences for nearly everyone, exacerbating the current situation. BrÃ¼ning and his cabinet believe that resolving the economic crisis internally is impossible; instead, they hope to achieve 'ignition'âaccelerating recovery once the crisis has passed
, through deliberately allowing the economic suffering to worsen to convince foreign powers that Germany couldn't afford to pay reparations. 
. This bitter medicine has guided all of BrÃ¼ning's economic policies, with the expectation that after the crisis, Germany will emerge stronger with lower labor costs
and a reduction in reparations
and the fiscal autonomy provided by the pause in reparations
.
Wilhelm Lautenbach, a German economist, has proposed a job creation program focused on expanding credit availability, improving liquidity, and investing in infrastructure. He presented this plan during in a secret meeting with economic ministers in BrÃ¼ningâs cabinet, independent experts, Reichsbank President Hans Luther, and our economic expert, Rudolf Hilferding, who personally supports BrÃ¼ning's deflationary approach. This is how we are able to listen on their secret discussions. However, the widespread fear of inflation in Germany has strongly influenced many participants against this policy, serving as the primary obstacle to the actual enactment of this plan.
Those in opposition to the deflationary policy and are open to public works include the

Lausanne Conference
At the Lausanne Conference, the Allies met with German representatives to resolve the issue of war reparations once and for all. Given the ongoing economic crisis, they concluded that Germany would be unable to resume payments after the moratorium ends.
Therefore, our reparations have been reduced by 90%! In the end, Germany is required to make only a final payment of three billion marks, effectively ending future reparation obligations. This has greatly boosted investor confidence and sparked a new wave of capital inflows to Germany. This should reduce the strain on our budget.

Hindenburg's Announcement
The President is to announce whether or not he will run again.
Hindenburg's Announcement
President Hindenburg has expressed his reluctance to run for re-election. At 84 years old, he prefers to spend his remaining years in retirement free from the burdens of his high office. While he has been open to serving longer if his term were extended by the Reichstag, the failure of those efforts, combined with his unwillingness to endure a grueling presidential campaign, has caused concern, particularly for Kurt von Schleicher
and Chancellor 
, on whom he depends to enact his decrees
.
Heinrich Sahm, the Mayor of Berlin, has formed a re-election committee to persuade Hindenburg to run again. This effort has included the collection of over 
 million signatures and appeals from a wide array of figures and political parties across German society, including Artur Mahraun of the Young German Order, and controversial former Defense Ministers Otto Gessler and Gustav Noske.
Impressed by the public support, Hindenburg is reconsidering his decision but insists that his candidacy must be backed by the German Right and veteran organizations rather than the German Left and Center. 
Hitler
Goebbels
, Hugenberg, their parties, and the <span style="color: #3E88B3;">

+2 resources

The Reichsbanner Is Banned!
Our so-called "revolutionary militia" is now officially outlawed nationwide.
Reichsbanner Banned
Following months of state repression, the hollowed-out remnants of the Reichsbanner are officially banned. With the more moderate fighters having retired or been absorbed into the stateâs militias, only die-hard <span style="color: #c00000;">socialists</span> remain in the self-defense organization. If this ban goes through, we will be left defenseless against state oppression. Many in the party urge us to resist, while others claim that the era of paramilitaries is over and argue for preemptive disarmament to placate Schleicher.
Ferdinand von Bredow - <span style="color: #BFC8CC;">
DNEF
</span>
Deputy Defense Minister. Ideology suspected to be similar to Schleicher's.
Presenting himself as a continuation of Schleicher and as another non-partisan military man, von Bredow has won his close friend Schleicher's favor. Although former chancellor Schleicher is officially retired, he still pulls many strings behind the scenes, unwilling to give up that scheming of his.
However, von Bredow is certainly no Schleicher, he possesses no vast network of political connections throughout all walks of life, and Schleicher's personal popularity has largely failed to transfer over to his hand picked successor.
Perhaps the house of cards Schleicher has carefully built will come tumbling down?
Schleicher's Announces His Successor
Schleicher's Successor
With his resignation announced last week, speculation over who will succeed the monumental leader now dominates the minds of citizens across the nation. Schleicher requests for a decree dissolving the Reichstag, pointing out the chamber in all likelihood approve a no-confidence motion at the end in the next reconvocation. Hindenburg remains unresponsive. His personal estrangement from Schleicher is already far advanced, and there have been little personal contact between the two men for a majority of Schleicher's term. Schleicher advocating for the same course of action that he himself opposed when it was advocated by Papen.
In the end, Hindenburg is only willing to provide a dissolution decree, but then he refuses to provide it to Schleicher as of right now. But to the public, Schleicher's hold on the chancellorship remains firm.
Schleicher resigns.
Schleicher returns to his waiting ministers and relays the outcome. An announcement of his resignationâprepared weeks in advanceâis released to the press.

 seem to be convinced that it is necessary for the Reichsbanner to be militarized, and mute their criticisms. 
Julius Curtius has surprisingly won the vote to succeed Stresemann for the chairmanship. The right-wing of the party have created a special vice-chairmanship position to maintain their influence on the party however. The Reichstag delegation are also able to act independently with Scholz remaining as their leader.
Nonetheless, this is still excellent news for us and our relations with the <span style="color: #C0A054;">
DVP
</span>. 
We have noticed a decrease in coalition tensions.
The <span style="color: #c00000;">
Iron
</span> <span style="color: #1A1A1A;">
Front
</span> rallies against nationalism and war.

Adolf Hitler is in power, but we are fighting to stop him.
Adolf Hitler has taken power in Germany. However, we have rallied the democratic forces of the country against him, and are fighting back. The outcome of this war is uncertain.
The <span style="color: #000000;">We</span><span style="color: #DD0000;">im</span><span style="color: #FFCC00;">ar</span> Republic Limps Forward
Ending 2
Following the scarring experience of the <span style="color: #7A3C00;">Nazi</span> movementâs rapid rise and implosion, the flaws of the <span style="color: #000000;">We</span><span style="color: #DD0000;">im</span><span style="color: #FFCC00;">ar</span> system became impossible to ignore. But in the end, the <span style="color: #7A3C00;">fascists</span> and <span style="color: #700000;">communists</span> who once battled in the streets now stand together in soup lines. Though a number of neo-<span style="color: #7A3C00;">Nazi</span> movements would emerge after 
âs mask of sanity slipped, the ideology itself remains irrevocably discredited.
No war as destructive as the Great War would come again. Slowly, Germany and the <span style="color: #0A3161;">Western</span> <span style="color: #C8102E;">Allies</span> began to rebuild relations, choosing to forget and forgive. <span style="color: #7A3C00;">Fascism</span> never takes root as a dominant force, and the primary threat to European democracy becomes the resurgent <span style="color: #750e0e;">Soviet Union</span>. The Spanish Civil War ends in 1936 with a Republican victory. 
In 1939, Austria's <span style="color: #7A3C00;">Austrofascist</span> regime collapses under popular revolt.
In 1938, Austria's ruling <span style="color: #A50034;">
SDAPÃ
</span> successfully holds a referendum confirming their <i>Anschluss</i> into Germany. 
The war in Austria is eventually won by the <span style="color: #7A3C00;">Austrofascists</span>, who receive generous aid from a <span style="color: #7A3C00;">fascist</span> Italy.
  Isolated and denied any success in his expansionist aims in Yugoslavia and Greece, Mussolini is deposed in a royalist coup led by King Victor Emmanuel III, and Italy returns to democratic governance
, leading to Austriaâs government democratizing as a consequence of losing its benefactor
.
Africa remains colonized until the 1970s and 80s. Independence, when it comes, is negotiated largely on the terms of the colonizers. Exploitation persists under a different guise. The Cold War unfolds along different lines, as a "state capitalist" <span style="color: #750e0e;">Soviet Union</span> remains intact, forming a bloc with the authoritarian <span style="color: #000099;">Kuomintang</span>-ruled Republic of China and post-independence India, which fought a bloody war to escape <span style="color: #C8102E;">British</span> rule in the 1950s. This tri-polar alliance has solidified into a global axis of its own.
Today, Germany is governed by a stable two-and-a-half party system, rotating power between a non-<span style="color: #9B0000;">Marxist</span> <span style="color: #c00000;">
SPD
</span>, the centrist <span style="color: #000000;">
CVP
</span>, and the liberal <span style="color: #FFCC00;">
LDPD
</span>. However, a surging <span style="color: #D79E2A;">
NDPD
</span> has emerged on the far right, capitalizing on public discontent over a refugee crisis driven by wars in an impoverished and unstable Africa.
Timeline of the SPD and the Republic (until 1928)
1875
: The SPD is formed as a merger of the General German Workers' Association, founded by Ferdinand Lassalle, and the Social Democratic Workers' Party of Germany, led by Wilhelm Liebknecht and August Bebel. Eventually, the SPD grows to become the largest party in the German Empire.
1914
: The Great War begins. The SPD votes in favor of war credits, with substantial internal dissent. This begins a policy of 
Burgfriedenspolitik
, a general peace between all political parties and trade unions.
1917
: The USPD, a left-wing anti-war split from the SPD, is formed.
1918
: Following defeats in the war and uprisings by soldiers, sailors, and workers, SPD leader Philip Scheidemann proclaims the German Republic, and Kaiser Wilhelm II abdicates from his throne. An armistice is declared, ending the Great War. Conflict between center-left social democrats and far-left radicals erupts in the streets of Berlin and elsewhere.
1919
: The Spartacist Uprising, in which communist workers attempt to seize control of government, is launched. The uprising is defeated by the Social Democratic government, now led by President Friedrich Ebert and Chancellor Philipp Scheidemann, with the support of the far-right Freikorps militas. The two key leaders of the uprising, Rosa Luxemburg and Karl Liebknecht, are murdered by the Freikorps. The Treaty of Versailles is signed under great protest from all political factions in Germany, officially ending the Great War. The Weimar Constitution is signed. The first "Weimar Coalition" government is formed, led by the SPD, DDP, and Center (Z) parties.
1920
: The Kapp Putsch, an attempt by reactionary actors within the military to take power, is launched, and is defeated by the workers with a general strike. This is followed by left-wing uprisings in the Ruhr, which are defeated by the Reichswehr and Freikorps. The Weimar Coalition is defeated in the elections, with the right and far left increasing in vote share. Elected governments in this period are very unstable, often only lasting a matter of months. There is a split in the USPD, with the majority faction joining with the KPD and the Soviet Union's Communist International (Comintern) alliance.
1921
: Communist uprisings in Saxony and Hamburg are defeated. Matthias Erzberger, the Center Party leader who signed the Versailles Treaty, is assassinated.
1922
: The Treaty of Rapallo is signed, which normalized relations with the Soviet Union. Walther Rathenau, the DDP Foreign Minister, is assassinated. The SPD reunites with most of the remaining USPD.
1923
: Hyperinflation begins, largely due to the economic policy of "passive resistance" against the French occupation of the Ruhr and the Versailles reparations. The currency is rapidly devalued as prices for necessities rise to billions of Reichsmarks. The government launches a <i>Reichsexekution</i> against Saxony, which was led by a joint SPD-KPD government. The Nazi "Beer Hall Putsch" is launched in Munich, and fails, with the arrest of Hitler. Eventually, new policies manage to stabilize the currency, and the French occupation ends.
1924
: Parliamentary elections are held in May and December, the latter leading to the bourgeois-right governments that last until 1928. Hitler is released from prison. The Dawes Plan for the repayment of the Versailles reparations is negotiated, which reduces payments and increases economic growth. Germany begins to enter a period of relative stability, with the end of the hyperinflation crisis.
1925
: President Friedrich Ebert dies, and new presidential elections are held, leading to the narrow election of Paul von Hindenburg in the second round. Hindenburg is a war hero and a right-wing reactionary. The SPD adopts the Heidelberg Program as its party platform. The Locarno Treaties are signed, which confirmed Germany's post-war western borders.
1926
: A referendum on the expropriation of the princes, supported by the SPD and KPD, fails.
Back
FAQ
Hindenburg keeps sacking me! How do I prevent it?
It's hard to prevent him from sacking you if you plan to stay in office for the full four years, but pausing reparations (by not exposing rearmament), keeping the Reichswehr loyal, maintaining a budget surplus, keeping a high pro republic support, a low coalition dissent, avoiding land reform, and keeping unemployment and NSDAP poll numbers low, all help. Remember, him sacking you is not the end of the world, and being free from the burden of government could be a blessing in disguise. Sometimes you have to accept it.
How impactful is government burden?
A main problem with newer players is that, they overestimate the negative effects of not completing government burden. It is not the end of the world if you miss a goal or two or three, some sacrifices are needed if you need to garner the budget for an economic plan. That said, you also can't afford to completely ignore the Labor Ministry; repeated failures there will significantly increase internal dissent.
How do I impact a party's ideologies and get certain paths? Is it RNG?
Nothing in Dynamic relies on RNG, aside from card draws. The Support for the Republic value plays a crucial role in determining party congress outcomes, particularly within the <span style="color: #3E88B3;">
DNVP
</span>. The liberal parties, on the other hand, are primarily influenced by governmental actionsâmoving toward economic democracy and left-wing policies will push them rightward, while maintaining good relations and avoiding major socialist policies will bring them closer. Relations also matter significantly, making the negotiation skills of MÃ¼ller and Braun highly valuable.
What happened to the WTB Plan?
Woytinsky only came up with the plan in late 1931 after the Banking Crisis, so to represent this, you need to get the event where Woytinsky proposes the concept in order to support it. The event is dynamic, and can trigger depending on factors such as: the state of the economy, deflation, labor faction strength, and most importantly internal party dissent. Appointing Woytinsky as an advisor aids significantly, as his presence passively increases the rate.
What is Dynamic Mode?
Dynamic mode is the intended way to play the mod, combining elements of both easy and historical modes. It reduces RNG with the shuffle leadership and card dismissal features of easy mode while retaining the starting variables of historical mode, such as relations and Reichsbanner strength.
How do I elect Stegerwald?
You will have to get Stegerwald as the leader of Prussia, either through Braun resigning, or having a right-wing coalition form.
How do I cross the rubicon?
Hindenburg needs to dismiss Chancellor BrÃ¼ning following the 1932 Presidential elections. This unlocks Rubicon Mode, which remains active for the rest of the game. Since this is the historical sequence of events, you'll need to follow a similar political path, such as securing a Hugenberg-led <span style="color: #3E88B3;">
DNVP
</span>, to have a better chance of unlocking it.
Why is the left path so hard?
Unlike Redux, this mod does not include any postâcivil war content or additional <span style="color: #700000;">
KPD
</span> or revolutionary paths. This is primarily due to time constraintsâby the time of release, the mod had already been in development for over 9 months. As a result, the only truly "viable" path is the Reformist one, given the immense difficulty of building socialism within a bourgeois democracyâespecially with figures like Hindenburg as president and the hostility of the bourgeois parties.
Why can't I form a coalition with a moderate Christian-Socialist DNVP or KVP?
The Christian Socials of the DNVP, and the party as a whole, remain a racist and antisemitic party, though they are more willing to operate within a democratic framework. The "Christian Socialism" of Weimar Germany is not the Christian Socialism of today; a better comparison would be Austriaâs Christian Social Party. It is a nationalist, corporatist, and deeply religious right-wing ideology. The KVP is still a strongly anti-Marxist and nationalistic party at heart, and it would be against everything they stand for to coalition with the socialists.

Judiciary Reform
The Judiciary is one of the most <span style="color: #808080;">reactionary</span> components of the government, along with the military. Many jurists have held office since the days of the Emperor. They give violent <span style="color: #7A3C00;">fascists</span> like Adolf Hitler laughably light sentences, while ruthlessly persecuting leftists and pacifists for lesser crimes.
There is great demand in our membership for reforming the judiciary, although this may bring us into a collision course with our coalition allies. 
We have started on the path to judicial reform. While some of the most antediluvian and biased justices have been replaced, many still remain. More should be done. 
We have successfully reformed the judiciary, to some extent. At the very least, the courts will not actively obstruct our attempts to prosecute crimes committed by the <span style="color: #7A3C00;">far</span>-<span style="color: #3E88B3;">right</span>.
Both sides need to share the pain in the current crisis.
Many workers are unhappy at our compromises, but they will learn to accept that we are all in a shared struggle against economic forces.
Labor Affairs

Labor Affairs
The industrial Ruhr region is aflame with labor unrest. The employers have started a major lockout to force the workers to take more cuts to wages and benefits. There is a need for the Labor Ministry to arbitrate in conflicts between employers and workers.
Could we induce more hiring and reduce unemployment by reducing work hours?
Some in the labor movement argue that by reducing the maximum number of hours worked, employers would be induced to hire more workers, reducing unemployment. The argument also goes that this would be complementary to deflation, maintaining the same purchasing power for workers by cutting hours without cutting wages. Employers, however, resist this policy, arguing for more flexibility in hiring and firing.
Corruption in big business is rampant, of course. We have uncovered a number of scandals, and publicizing them brings new attention to the work of Interior Minister 
, building up his popular appeal and that of the <span style="color: #c00000;">
SPD
</span>.

All Quiet on the Western Front
The American film 
All Quiet on the Western Front
, based on the German novel 
Im Westen nichts Neues
 by Erich Maria Remarque, is a film about the experiences of German soldiers in the Great War, and how they gradually became disillusioned with the ideas of patriotism and nationalism.
As an anti-war film, it is the target of major protest by <span style="color: #7A3C00;">far</span>-<span style="color: #3E88B3;">right</span> actors, especially the <span style="color: #7A3C00;">Nazis</span> and the <span style="color: #3E88B3;">
Stahlhelm
</span>. They are calling for the government to ban the film. If we do not ban it, the <span style="color: #7A3C00;">
SA
</span> threatens to violently stop the film from being shown or watched.
Since we lack control over the Prussian state government, the decision to allow or ban the film will ultimately rest with the bourgeois parties.

Both Hugenberg and Hergt lack the majority of votes needed within the party, and now are seeking a compromise solution between the two factions. The failure of the Young Plan referendum to reach the 10% threshold
The all-or-nothing policy of Hugenberg leaving the party unable to enter government and revert our 
land reform policies
and 
tariff reductions
, and the declining popularity of the <span style="color: #3E88B3;">
DNVP
</span> has convinced the majority of the party to vote out Hugenberg. He is replaced with Treviranus, a member of the "Young Conservative" wing of the party. The Young Conservatives are in favor of governmental participation, advancing their goals in the framework of the system. However, the national conservatives retain heavy control over the party and the <span style="color: #3E88B3;">

</span> isn't exactly a democratic party yet.

DNVP
</span>, especially since he has worked to eliminate moderate influences within the party organizations, who are likely to follow him.
The moderates within the <span style="color: #3E88B3;">
DNVP
</span> united under Westarp are unable to defeat Hugenberg in a vote. This is in part due to Hugenberg's efforts of consolidating power within the party organizations. The <span style="color: #385A38;">agrarian</span> representatives in the party finally had enough with Hugenberg's "all or nothing" strategy, especially after he governed as de-facto party dictator, disregarding even those he has shared power with.
The anti-Hugenberg forces within the party have all left to form a new <i>Konservative Volkspartei</i> (Conservative People's Party). It is a question of how long this party would last, with some members rather wanting to affiliate with the special interest parties. Hugenberg is left with a diminished <span style="color: #3E88B3;">
DNVP
</span>, smaller than the faction that split from it.
The party prefers to elect Schiele.
The <span style="color: #3E88B3;">
DNVP
</span>âs shift toward <span style="color: #6D5298;">Christian Corporatism</span> and <span style="color: #000000;">repu</span><span style="color: #DD0000;">blic</span><span style="color: #FFCC00;">anism</span> has stalled with the re-election of Kuno von Westarp as party leader, promoted from his role as chairman of the Reichstag delegation. So far, Westarp has avoided drastic changes to prevent a party split, frustrating the partyâs more conservative and industrial backers, who had hoped his leadership would restore the <span style="color: #3E88B3;">
DNVP
</span> to its previous course.
At the same time, Westarp is careful not to alienate the <span style="color: #6D5298;">Christian Social</span> wing through drastic changes to the party program, fearing a split or the defection of its working-class base back to the <span style="color: #c00000;">
SPD
</span>. As a compromise, he preserves some worker-friendly provisions in the party program and messaging. While this is not enough to hold onto the working-class support Lambach had built, the party offsets these losses by winning back monarchist and conservative voters from the <span style="color: #003755;">
DNF
</span>, aided by a resurgence in industrial funding.
The <span style="color: #385A38;">agrarian</span> wing isn't too displeased with Treviranus.
Due to the land reform policies enacted by our
&nbsp;previous
 government, the <span style="color: #385A38;">agrarian</span> conservative elites have made it their strongest effort to rally the German right against them. This involves orchestrating a leadership change within the <span style="color: #3E88B3;">
DNVP
</span>, the primary political home of <span style="color: #385A38;">agrarian</span> conservatism. Treviranus has been ousted in a vote of no confidence, in favor of the president of the National Rural League (RLB), Martin Schiele. By electing the most influential figure in the agricultural community, they aim to safeguard the role of organized agriculture in Germany, viewing Treviranus as too aligned with middle class interests. 
With the <span style="color: #c00000;">
SPD
</span> making significant inroads among rural voters, the <span style="color: #3E88B3;">
DNVP
</span> and its <span style="color: #385A38;">agrarian</span> elites have ousted Treviranus in a vote of no confidence, to elect Martin Schiele, the president of the National Rural League (RLB). They believe Schiele, the most influential figure in the agricultural community, can halt this shift and preserve the longstanding alliance between agriculture and German conservatism, seeing Treviranus as too aligned with middle-class interests. 
Due to the significant inroads made by the insurgent <span style="color: #7A3C00;">
NSDAP
</span> among their primary rural voter base, the <span style="color: #3E88B3;">
DNVP
</span> and its <span style="color: #385A38;">agrarian</span> elites have ousted Treviranus in a vote of no confidence, to elect Martin Schiele, president of the National Rural League (RLB). They believe Schiele, the most influential figure in the agricultural community, can halt this shift and preserve the longstanding alliance between agriculture and German conservatism, seeing Treviranus as too aligned with middle-class interests. 
Schieleâs election has led to the merger of the <span style="color: #7FCEB1;">
CNBLP
</span> into the <span style="color: #3E88B3;">
DNVP
</span>, strengthening its hold on the countryside and uniting the RLB in lobbying for <span style="color: #385A38;">agrarian</span> interests. This consolidation has given rise to the renewal of the <span style="color: #385A38;">Green Front</span>, an umbrella group of the RLB, peasant unions, and some Catholic and 
<span style="color: #000000;">Center</span>
<span style="color: #000000;">

</span>
-affiliated agricultural organizations.

The victory of moderates within the <span style="color: #3E88B3;">
DNVP
</span> has not entirely stopped the partyâs rightward shift since its electoral defeat years ago. The surprise election of Gottfried Treviranus briefly reversed this trend, but the worsening economic crisis has swung the pendulum in favor of the partyâs conservatives once again. Additionally, Treviranus's ambivalence on monarchism has sparked controversy, deepening divisions within the partyâthis time creating a genuine split, unlike the debate over Lambachâs article years prior.
While the turmoil from Hugenbergâs split and the Young Plan has subsided, a further rightward shift remains possible, especially as the <span style="color: #7A3C00;">
NSDAP
</span> threatens to eclipse them. Treviranus now faces a battle against the partyâs <span style="color: #808080;">reactionary</span> elements as he works to modernize the <span style="color: #3E88B3;">
DNVP
</span> and secure its democratic future.
Stresemann and the left-wing of the <span style="color: #C0A054;">
DVP
</span> pushed through the reform by an extremely narrow margin, only achieved by Stresemann's threats to resign if it were not to pass. Half of the party's Reichstag delegation is tied to industry, which are now without much influence within the <span style="color: #C0A054;">
DVP
</span>, and have mostly shifted to support the <span style="color: #3E88B3;">
DNVP
</span> now. Those in the Reichstag delegation are now in open conflict with the left-wing of the <span style="color: #C0A054;">

Kellogg-Briand Pact
An international treaty has been negotiated which promises an end to war. The treaty, negotiated by American Secretary of State Frank Kellogg and French foreign minister Aristide Briand, promises that its signatories will renounce the use of war, and will promise to settle their disputes peacefully. Countries throughout the world are lining up to sign this idealistic agreement. Its detractors argue that it has no enforcement mechanism, and thus is merely a worthless piece of paper.

Prussian Landtag referendum
After months of intense and polarizing propaganda from both sides, the day of the plebiscite has arrived. Emergency Decree Request
Following the Camarillaâs meeting with Papen and

The course Schleicher pursues is one of three options developed by his colleagues in the Defense Ministry. The first two involve unconstitutional maneuvers: either postponing elections or forcing the Reichstag into indefinite adjournment.
The third option, however, is more intriguingâa constructive vote of no confidence. Legal advisors have identified a loophole in the Republicâs constitution: while a hostile Reichstag majority can pass a no-confidence vote, it cannot unite to support a new cabinet. In the event of <span style="color: #7A3C00;">

This option is favored within the Defense Ministry: it has the potential to attract <span style="color: #000000;">rep</span><span style="color: #DD0000;">ubl</span><span style="color: #FFCC00;">ican</span> support and avoid the civil unrest that the other two would likely provoke. Confronting Antisemitism

Subending A - The World's Most Unstable Republic
The political deadlock that defined the gravest crisis of the <span style="color: #000000;">We</span><span style="color: #DD0000;">im</span><span style="color: #FFCC00;">ar</span> Republic has persisted over the years. The <span style="color: #3E88B3;">
DNVP
</span> has never been fully reconciled with a system it was fundamentally opposed to from the start. SPD
</span>.
The presidency has remained out of reach for the <span style="color: #c00000;">
SPD
</span>, with successors to Hindenburg's worldview continuing to preside over Germany and obstruct our return to executive power. In 1939, a recessionâsparked by European instability and rising tensions with the <span style="color: #750e0e;">Soviet Union</span>âtriggered another wave of extremism. The Reichswehr was forced to step in to prevent a "March on Berlin" by both <span style="color: #700000;">communists</span> and <span style="color: #7A3C00;">fascism</span>.
Though real democracy was formally restored in the 1960s following massive public discontent, the <span style="color: #6A1E1E;">
DSU
</span> has remained a persistent force on the far-right. Recently rebranding itself as the <span style="color: #D79E2A;">

</span>, it has capitalized on growing public anger over a refugee crisis driven by continued war and instability across Africa.

State Buyer
-2 budget - Create a state monopoly for grain purchases, guaranteeing prices for farmers and stable prices for consumers. 
The judiciary would never allow this.
Our new purchasing agency guarantees fair prices for farmers, while also providing lower food prices for workers and the urban poor. This policy necessitates heavy spending and an unprecedented level of state intervention in the economy, raising the ire of conservative economists.
This policy has earned us the ire of President Hindenburg and his inner circle.
Agricultural Policy
Do not enact any policies for now.
Agricultural Policy
While agricultural policy has historically not been the domain of the <span style="color: #c00000;">
SPD
</span>, there is much need for policy in this area. Our urban constituencies hope for cheaper and more stable food supplies, while small farmers need economic relief. There is also the movement for land reform, to break up the large aristocratic estates and redistribute land to peasants.
-2 resources
We do not have enough resources. 
The <span style="color: #C0A054;">
DVP
</span> doesn't want our resources anymore. 
The <span style="color: #FFCC00;">
LVP
</span> doesn't want our resources anymore.
By transferring campaign funds to our coalition partners, we can make our policies more acceptable to them. Some would call this corruption, but perhaps it is necessary to save democracy.
Votes of no confidence can only be called if an alternative plan for government formation can be passed, greatly reducing their incidence.

The <span style="color: #000000;">Center Party</span>'s middle class wing are in uproar over the vote, despite Stegerwald conceding his leadership of the Reichstag delegation and the Minister-Presidency to his protege Heinrich BrÃ¼ning. 
His election also attracts some Protestant <span style="color: #3E88B3;">
DNVP
</span> dissidents and industrialists, who may result in a shift of the party towards the right.

Konrad Adenauer, the long-time Mayor of Cologne, has been elevated to the leadership of one of Germany's largest parties, which he strongly supported the formation of. Adenauer is committed to democratic principles and sits on the right wing of the former <span style="color: #000000;">Center</span>, without being tied to Christian labor. This has granted him the ability to rally a broad coalition of party leaders around his calm and stabilizing leadership against the more fringe politicians of the party.
With his slogan 'No Experiments!', we do not expect any major changes to the party program under his leadership, fostering a sense among the people that the <span style="color: #000000;">
</span> is a pole of stability that the German people can rally behind. Even in his current state, Schleicher still holds influence over the party.
The two factions within the <span style="color: #BFC8CC;">
DNEF
</span> have effectively undergone a split, their differences proving too vast to reconcile. With no resolution in sight, the minority faction has broken away, taking a segment of the party with them.
This unlucky minority is the <span style="color: #BFC8CC;">
DNEF
</span>âs right wing, which sought to transform the party into a vehicle for the restoration of the German monarchy. The ideological gulf was too great for the ailing Schleicher to bridge, leading to the formation of the <span style="color: #BFC8CC;">DNEF-Fatherland League</span> (<span style="color: #BFC8CC;">
DNEF-VLB
</span>)âperhaps an echo of the German Fatherland Party of old. 
This unlucky minority is the <span style="color: #BFC8CC;">
DNEF
</span>âs left wing, which hoped to redirect the party toward addressing social inequality and defending the welfare of the German worker. Disputes over monarchism and the role of labor in the new corporatist order proved irreconcilable for Schleicher, resulting in their secession and the founding of the <span style="color: #B86558;">DNEF-Social Bloc</span> (<span style="color: #B86558;">
DNEF-SB
</span>). 
However, what is fascinating is that both factions now claim to be the âtrueâ <span style="color: #BFC8CC;">
DNEF
</span>. Chancellor 
 has so far avoided taking sides, hoping to prevent further splintering of an already fragile alliance. Perhaps this is Schleicherâs influence at workâa political truce between two perfectly normal, merely âdisagreeingâ factions.

"MEFO Bills"
Ordered by Hjalmar Schacht of the Economic Ministry.
Despite Hjalmar Schacht not being a Keynesian, he understands that large scale public works and state funded projected are the way out of Germany's slump, and for Germany to retake its great power status on the international stage. He believes that, since <span style="color: #002654;">France</span> and <span style="color: #C8102E;">Britain</span> are preoccupied with their own economic woes, the time is right for a mass industrialization and rearmament program.
The construction of the "Autobahn", a new nationwide highway system, by poorly paid workers has begun, as an expansion of Schleicher's public works plans. A new innovative method for disguising rearmament has also been devised, a "MEFO bill", a system of promissory notes issued through a dummy company to secretly fund arms production while keeping the spending off the official government budget. However, he insists that a revival of Germany's economy should take precedent over rearmament.
Unfortunately, the coalition supporting the dissolution of the Reichstag does not constitute a majority. There will be elections as scheduled.

Understanding the Enemy

Understanding the Enemy
The <span style="color: #7A3C00;">Nazis</span>' rise is unprecedented for a political party in modern Germany, and <span style="color: #c00000;">socialist</span> intellectuals are doing their best to come to terms with the rapid rise of this dangerous party.
The trade union researcher Theodor Geiger has written an article titled "Panik im Mittelstand", in which he describes the <span style="color: #7A3C00;">Nazis</span> as being primarily a movement of the middle class, especially lower-middle civil servants and professionals who fear the proletarianizing effects of the Depression. Geiger argues that the middle class has been economically and politically unmoored, long split between many competing parties, squeezed between the big businesses and the proletariat. The mix of nationalist, anti-democratic, and loosely anti-capitalist rhetoric of the <span style="color: #7A3C00;">Nazis</span> appeals to this group, perhaps only temporarily before the middle class discovers its true class interests.
By contrast, the theoretician Alexander Schifrin understands the <span style="color: #7A3C00;">Nazis</span> as a new, unprecedented type of political movement, a <span style="color: #7A3C00;">fascist</span> party that has non-economic appeal across class lines. He calls to attention its use of violent propaganda and street demonstrations as central to their rise.
Carlo Mierendorff, a leader of the "new revisionists", understands the <span style="color: #7A3C00;">Nazis</span> as a politically incoherent but nevertheless powerful movement. They are kept together by the strength of their organizing machine and the emotional appeal of their nebulous ideology, which is able to attract less informed voters who voted based on feeling. Countering them would require a new style of campaigning, focused more on symbols and emotion rather than reason.
It is possible that all or none of these hypotheses are correct. Whatever the case, there must be something we can do to stop their rise.
We have accepted the compromise offered by the bourgeoisie. Many of the workers and unemployed see this as a betrayal, as we cut welfare without increasing employer contributions. We present it as the successful aversion of a much worse alternative.

The WeltbÃ¼hne Trial
The <span style="color: #c00000;">left-wing</span> news and literary magazine WeltbÃ¼hne has been targeted by the national authorities for allegedly revealing state secrets. The magazine published articles alleging a secret rearmament program by the Reichswehr, in contravention of the Treaty of Versailles. The military authorities obviously do not want this information to be known. It does not help that the magazine's editor, Carl von Ossietzky, is a well-known pacifist.

The Young Plan and the fall of the coalition
The Foreign Ministry under Gustav Stresemann has agreed to the Young Plan, an agreement with the Western powers to reduce Germany's reparation payments and give us more fiscal autonomy. Simultaneously, <span style="color: #002654;">France</span> will agree to withdraw its military occupation from the Rhineland.
While this seems to be a positive development for the country, the <span style="color: #3E88B3;">
DNVP

The <span style="color: #000000;">We</span><span style="color: #DD0000;">im</span><span style="color: #FFCC00;">ar</span> Republic Marches On
Ending 3
At the turn of the decade, the Great Depression and widespread disillusionment with the <span style="color: #000000;">We</span><span style="color: #DD0000;">im</span><span style="color: #FFCC00;">ar</span> system left few optimists in Germany. Yet, the Republicâforged by the will of the people all those years agoâhas weathered a crisis of unprecedented proportions. <span style="color: #c00000;">Social Democracy</span> endures, as do its detractors.
No war as destructive as the Great War would come again. Slowly, Germany and the <span style="color: #0A3161;">Western</span> <span style="color: #C8102E;">Allies</span> began to rebuild relations, choosing to forget and forgive. <span style="color: #7A3C00;">Fascism</span> never takes root as a dominant force, and the primary threat to European democracy becomes the resurgent <span style="color: #750e0e;">Soviet Union</span>. The Spanish Civil War ends in 1936 with a Republican victory. 
In 1939, Austria's <span style="color: #7A3C00;">Austrofascist</span> regime collapses under popular revolt.
In 1938, Austria's ruling <span style="color: #A50034;">
SDAPÃ
</span> successfully holds a referendum confirming their <i>Anschluss</i> into Germany. 
The war in Austria is eventually won by the <span style="color: #7A3C00;">Austrofascists</span>, who receive generous aid from a <span style="color: #7A3C00;">fascist</span> Italy.
 Isolated and denied any success in his expansionist aims in Yugoslavia and Greece, Mussolini is deposed in a royalist coup led by King Victor Emmanuel III, and Italy returns to democratic governance
, leading to Austriaâs government democratizing as a consequence of losing its benefactor
.
Africa remains colonized until the 1970s and 80s. Independence, when it comes, is negotiated largely on the terms of the colonizers. Exploitation persists under a different guise. The Cold War unfolds along different lines, as a "state capitalist" <span style="color: #750e0e;">Soviet Union</span> remains intact, forming a bloc with the authoritarian <span style="color: #000099;">Kuomintang</span>-ruled Republic of China and post-independence India, which fought a bloody war to escape <span style="color: #C8102E;">British</span> rule in the 1950s. This tri-polar alliance has solidified into a global axis of its own.

"Such a general development cannot be attributed to the false tactics of any single party or to the mistakes of individual leaders. On the contrary, the conduct of individual leaders is determined largely by the sentiments of the people as a whole. It would be erroneous, however, to regard the sentiments of the moment as reflecting the natural make-up and character of the people. They are merely the consequence of the special circumstances which have brought about this profound degradation of the entire nation."
- Karl Kautsky, 
Hitlerism and Social Democracy
, 1934
This is a game of alternate history. While historical accuracy is a major concern, keep in mind that this is a 
game
, and that any divergence from actual events will necessarily have to involve some element of make-believe. Nevertheless, please let the author know if there are any glaring mistakes.
For more information on the politics and government of the German Republic, see the "Library" page on the top left. If this is your first time playing the game, it is strongly recommended that you read at least some of this information.
If you are playing this mod via the Mod Loader in base game, there will be various items missing (parliament colours, images, arrows, etc.). For the intended experience, please play using <a href="https://originn0.github.io/dynamic_social_democracy/" style="color: #0077FF">this</a> link.

Economic Democracy
A key part of the <span style="color: #c00000;">social democratic</span> platform is that workers should have more control over the economy. This can be done either directly at the enterprise level, by giving works councils greater influence over management, or indirectly via the democratic government, by acquiring key industries from the capitalist class and placing them under democratic control.
As we control the Labor 
and Economic 
and Finance 
 ministries, we can finally begin to implement our long-standing goals.

Social Welfare Policy
The social welfare program, including unemployment insurance, healthcare, and old-age care, is the proudest achievement of the <span style="color: #c00000;">
SPD
</span> in our time in government. There is continuous pressure to dismantle the welfare system

</span> is planning a march through Altona, a working-class neighborhood of Hamburg known for its high working-class population. Altona is a <span style="color: #700000;">Communist</span> stronghold, and the planned march will almost certainly lead to violent conflict.
Can we bring the sides together for a peace?
The Austrian government obviously sees us as biased, so there is no way they will let us host peace negotiations. However, negotiations begin nonetheless. In Switzerland, with <span style="color: #7A3C00;">Fascist</span> Italy as the <span style="color: #7A3C00;">Austrofascists</span>' protector and Germany as the <span style="color: #A50034;">
SDAPÃ
</span>'s, a peace deal is hammered out. The <span style="color: #A50034;">socialists</span> are disarmed and give up all pretense of revolution, while the <span style="color: #ABDDA4;">right-wing</span> paramilitaries are also disarmed. Dictatorship ends, and normal government is restored, with free elections.
Given the <span style="color: #A50034;">
SDAPÃ
</span>'s starting position, this is treated as a victory. Now begins the long work of reconstruction...
